pathways.label: Non-conviction expungement for dismissal, no disposition, or acquittal
pathways.summary: You may have a non-conviction expungement pathway. The next step is to get the court disposition and file in the court where the case originated. Misdemeanor arrest/citation not formally charged or prosecuted within 12 months Under 99-15-59, a person arrested, cited, or held for a misdemeanor and not formally charged or prosecuted within 12 months of arrest, or whose charge was dismissed, may apply to the court with jurisdiction for expungement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-59/?utm_source=chatgpt.com") Was this a misdemeanor arrest or ticket where no formal charge or prosecution…
ruleClauses: You may have a non-conviction expungement pathway. The next step is to get the court disposition and file in the court where the case originated.
Misdemeanor arrest/citation not formally charged or prosecuted within 12 months

Under 99-15-59, a person arrested, cited, or held for a misdemeanor and not formally charged or prosecuted within 12 months of arrest, or whose charge was dismissed, may apply to the court with jurisdiction for expungement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-59/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Was this a misdemeanor arrest or ticket where no formal charge or prosecution happened within 12 months?
Dismissed, dropped, no disposition, or not guilty

Mississippi has a broad non-conviction pathway. Upon petition, the court shall expunge a case where an arrest was made, the person was released, and the case was dismissed, the charges were dropped, there was no disposition, or the person was found not guilty at trial. This appears in both 99-15-26(5) and 99-19-71(4). ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-26/") ("https://legiscan.com/MS/text/HB1546/2026")
Were you convicted, or was the case dismissed, dropped, no-billed, passed to file, never disposed of, or did you win at trial?
First-offender misdemeanor conviction

Under 99-19-71(1), a person convicted of a misdemeanor that is not a traffic violation, and who is a first offender, may petition the justice, county, circuit, or municipal court where the conviction occurred for expungement from all public records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/")
Was this your first conviction, and was it a non-traffic misdemeanor?
Misdemeanor conviction Must be misdemeanor, not felony
Not traffic Traffic misdemeanors generally do not use this path
First offender No prior conviction issue
File in convicting court Justice, county, circuit, or municipal court
Additional justice court or municipal court misdemeanors

Mississippi also has a separate discretionary pathway for additional misdemeanor convictions in justice court and municipal court. Under 9-11-15(3) and 21-23-7(6), the court may expunge any or all misdemeanors in that court if, after notice to the prosecutor and a showing in open court, the person proves:
2 Good conduct for 2 years since the last conviction in any court; and
3 Expungement would serve the best interest of society. ("https://msdeltada.com/expungement/")
Wilma should not treat this as automatic.
Some additional municipal or justice court misdemeanors may be expungeable, but this requires a court showing of rehabilitation, two years of good conduct, and the courts discretion.
General felony conviction expungement

Mississippi allows expungement of one felony conviction from public records if the person has paid all criminal fines and court costs and has completed all terms and conditions of the sentence. Current law states the waiting period is 5 yearsafter successful completion of sentence terms; HB 1546 changes that to 3 years effective July 1, 2026. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/") ("https://legiscan.com/MS/text/HB1546/2026")
Wilma should encode this as a date-sensitive rule:
Before July 1, 2026 5 years after successful completion of all sentence terms
On or after July 1, 2026 3 years after successful completion of all sentence terms

Mississippi has a special first-offense DUI expungement rule. A person convicted of a first-offense DUI may petition the circuit court of the county of conviction at least 5 years after successful completion of all sentence terms, if strict conditions are met. The person must not have held a CDL or commercial learners permit at the time of the offense, must not have refused the blood/breath test, must have had a BAC below .16 if test results are available, must have no other DUI conviction or pending DUI, must justify expungement, and must not have previously had a DUI nonadjudication or expungement. The Mississippi Attorney General has also stated that a municipal court judge is not authorized to expunge a DUI conviction, although municipal or justice courts may handle expungement of nonadjudicated first-offense DUI charges in certain circumstances. ("https://attorneygenerallynnfitch.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/P.Booker_September-29-2020-Expungement-of-Drug-Related-DUIs.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

Under 67-3-70, Mississippi has a special path for underage purchase or possession of light wine, light spirit product, or beer. External clearinghouse guidance summarizes that records relating to underage alcohol purchase/possession can be expunged by the court one year after sentence completion. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-67/chapter-3/section-67-3-70/?utm_source=chatgpt.com") ("https://nationalreentryresourcecenter.org/cleanslate/states/mississippi/policies/ms-c-7?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

Nonadjudication under 99-15-26

Mississippi courts may withhold acceptance of a guilty plea and sentence while the person completes court-imposed conditions. After successful completion, the court directs that the cause be dismissed and the case closed. Upon petition, the court expunges qualifying dismissed records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-26/")

This may apply in felony and misdemeanor cases, but there are exclusions, especially crimes against the person, crimes of violence, and certain public corruption/abuse-of-office offenses. The 4th District Attorneys expungement overview also treats misdemeanor and felony nonadjudication as a separate expungement pathway after successful completion. ("https://msdeltada.com/expungement/")
Was this a nonadjudication, deferred plea, or withheld adjudication where the court dismissed the case after you completed conditions?
Dismissed, dropped, no disposition, or not guilty

Mississippi has a broad non-conviction pathway. Upon petition, the court shall expunge a case where an arrest was made, the person was released, and the case was dismissed, the charges were dropped, there was no disposition, or the person was found not guilty at trial. This appears in both 99-15-26(5) and 99-19-71(4). ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-26/") ("https://legiscan.com/MS/text/HB1546/2026")
Were you convicted, or was the case dismissed, dropped, no-billed, passed to file, never disposed of, or did you win at trial?
You may have a non-conviction expungement pathway. The next step is to get the court disposition and file in the court where the case originated.

Under 99-15-59, a person arrested, cited, or held for a misdemeanor and not formally charged or prosecuted within 12 months of arrest, or whose charge was dismissed, may apply to the court with jurisdiction for expungement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-59/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

Under 99-19-71(1), a person convicted of a misdemeanor that is not a traffic violation, and who is a first offender, may petition the justice, county, circuit, or municipal court where the conviction occurred for expungement from all public records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/")

Mississippi allows expungement of one felony conviction from public records if the person has paid all criminal fines and court costs and has completed all terms and conditions of the sentence. Current law states the waiting period is 5 yearsafter successful completion of sentence terms; HB 1546 changes that to 3 years effective July 1, 2026. Mississippi has a special first-offense DUI expungement rule. A person convicted of a first-offense DUI may petition the circuit court of the county of conviction at least 5 years after successful completion of all sentence terms, if strict conditions are met. The person must not have held a CDL or commercial learners permit at the time of the offense, must not have refused the blood/breath test, must have had a BAC below .16 if test results are available, must have no other DUI conviction or pending DUI, must justify expungement, and must not have previously had a DUI nonadjudication or expungement. The Mississippi Attorney General has also stated that a municipal court judge is not authorized to expunge a DUI conviction, although municipal or justice courts may handle expungement of nonadjudicated first-offense DUI charges in certain circumstances. ("https://attorneygenerallynnfitch.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/P.Booker_September-29-2020-Expungement-of-Drug-Related-DUIs.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

Mississippi intervention court completion can result in expungement. Under 9-23-23, if the participant completes all intervention court requirements, including fines and fees not waived by the court, the charge and prosecution are dismissed. Dismissed, dropped, no disposition, or not guilty

Mississippi has a broad non-conviction pathway. Upon petition, the court shall expunge a case where an arrest was made, the person was released, and the case was dismissed, the charges were dropped, there was no disposition, or the person was found not guilty at trial. This appears in both 99-15-26(5) and 99-19-71(4). ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-26/") ("https://legiscan.com/MS/text/HB1546/2026")

Misdemeanor arrest/citation not formally charged or prosecuted within 12 months

Under 99-15-59, a person arrested, cited, or held for a misdemeanor and not formally charged or prosecuted within 12 months of arrest, or whose charge was dismissed, may apply to the court with jurisdiction for expungement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-59/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

First-offender misdemeanor conviction

Under 99-19-71(1), a person convicted of a misdemeanor that is not a traffic violation, and who is a first offender, may petition the justice, county, circuit, or municipal court where the conviction occurred for expungement from all public records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/")

Mississippi also has a separate discretionary pathway for additional misdemeanor convictions in justice court and municipal court. Under 9-11-15(3) and 21-23-7(6), the court may expunge any or all misdemeanors in that court if, after notice to the prosecutor and a showing in open court, the person proves:
2 Good conduct for 2 years since the last conviction in any court; and
3 Expungement would serve the best interest of society. ("https://msdeltada.com/expungement/")
Wilma should not treat this as automatic.
Some additional municipal or justice court misdemeanors may be expungeable, but this requires a court showing of rehabilitation, two years of good conduct, and the courts discretion.

Mississippi expungement removes qualifying records from public records, but it is not absolute deletion from every possible system. Mississippi law allows the Mississippi Criminal Information Center to retain a nonpublic record only to determine future first-offender status, and district attorneys may retain a nonpublic record for law-enforcement purposes only. The statute also says an employer may still ask whether a prospective employee has had an expungement order entered. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/")

sourceSections.title: Core Mississippi Rule
sourceSections.text:
Mississippi expungement removes qualifying records from public records, but it is not absolute deletion from every possible system. Mississippi law allows the Mississippi Criminal Information Center to retain a nonpublic record only to determine future first-offender status, and district attorneys may retain a nonpublic record for law-enforcement purposes only. The statute also says an employer may still ask whether a prospective employee has had an expungement order entered. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/")
Wilma should say:

Mississippi expungement can remove an eligible arrest, charge, or conviction from public records. It does not mean every nonpublic government or law-enforcement record disappears, and some narrow legal exceptions remain.

sourceSections.title: Main Mississippi Expungement Pathways
sourceSections.text:
Path A - Dismissed, dropped, no disposition, or not guilty
Mississippi has a broad non-conviction pathway. Upon petition, the court shall expunge a case where an arrest was made, the person was released, and the case was dismissed, the charges were dropped, there was no disposition, or the person was found not guilty at trial. This appears in both 99-15-26(5) and 99-19-71(4). ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-26/") ("https://legiscan.com/MS/text/HB1546/2026")
Wilma intake question:

Were you convicted, or was the case dismissed, dropped, no-billed, passed to file, never disposed of, or did you win at trial?

If no conviction:

You may have a non-conviction expungement pathway. The next step is to get the court disposition and file in the court where the case originated.

Path B - Misdemeanor arrest/citation not formally charged or prosecuted within 12 months
Under 99-15-59, a person arrested, cited, or held for a misdemeanor and not formally charged or prosecuted within 12 months of arrest, or whose charge was dismissed, may apply to the court with jurisdiction for expungement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-59/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma should ask:

Was this a misdemeanor arrest or ticket where no formal charge or prosecution happened within 12 months?

Path C - First-offender misdemeanor conviction
Under 99-19-71(1), a person convicted of a misdemeanor that is not a traffic violation, and who is a first offender, may petition the justice, county, circuit, or municipal court where the conviction occurred for expungement from all public records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/")
Wilma should ask:

Was this your first conviction, and was it a non-traffic misdemeanor?

Basic rule:

Requirement Wilma Logic
Misdemeanor conviction Must be misdemeanor, not felony
Not traffic Traffic misdemeanors generally do not use this path
First offender No prior conviction issue
File in convicting court Justice, county, circuit, or municipal court

Path D - Additional justice court or municipal court misdemeanors
Mississippi also has a separate discretionary pathway for additional misdemeanor convictions in justice court and municipal court. Under 9-11-15(3) and 21-23-7(6), the court may expunge any or all misdemeanors in that court if, after notice to the prosecutor and a showing in open court, the person proves:

1 Rehabilitation;
2 Good conduct for 2 years since the last conviction in any court; and
3 Expungement would serve the best interest of society. ("https://msdeltada.com/expungement/")
Wilma should not treat this as automatic.

Some additional municipal or justice court misdemeanors may be expungeable, but this requires a court showing of rehabilitation, two years of good conduct, and the courts discretion.

Path E - General felony conviction expungement
Mississippi allows expungement of one felony conviction from public records if the person has paid all criminal fines and court costs and has completed all terms and conditions of the sentence. Current law states the waiting period is 5 yearsafter successful completion of sentence terms; HB 1546 changes that to 3 years effective July 1, 2026. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/") ("https://legiscan.com/MS/text/HB1546/2026")
Wilma should encode this as a date-sensitive rule:

Filing Date Waiting Period
Before July 1, 2026 5 years after successful completion of all sentence terms
On or after July 1, 2026 3 years after successful completion of all sentence terms
The court may grant the felony petition if it determines, on the record or in writing, that the applicant is rehabilitated. The petitioner must give 10 days written notice to the district attorney before any hearing. (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/")
Felony conviction exclusions
A person is not eligible for general felony conviction expungement if the felony is classified as:

Excluded Felony Category
Crime of violence under 97-3-2
First-degree arson
Trafficking in controlled substances
Third, fourth, or subsequent DUI
Felon in possession of a firearm
Failure to register as a sex offender
Voyeurism
Witness intimidation
Abuse, neglect, or exploitation of a vulnerable person
Embezzlement
Effective July 1, 2026: felony procuring prostitution
Effective July 1, 2026: promoting prostitution
The new prostitution-related exclusions come from HB 1546, effective July 1, 2026. (

"https://legiscan.com/MS/text/HB1546/2026")
Wilma should say:

Mississippi allows only one general felony conviction expungement. The exact charge and statute must be checked before eligibility is marked.

Path F - Public official bar
No public official is eligible for expungement under 99-19-71 for any conviction related to official duties. ("https://legiscan.com/MS/text/HB1546/2026")
Wilma should ask:

Were you a public official, and was the conviction connected to official duties?

If yes, escalate.

Path G - Nonadjudication under 99-15-26
Mississippi courts may withhold acceptance of a guilty plea and sentence while the person completes court-imposed conditions. After successful completion, the court directs that the cause be dismissed and the case closed. Upon petition, the court expunges qualifying dismissed records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/in-general/section-99-15-26/")
This may apply in felony and misdemeanor cases, but there are exclusions, especially crimes against the person, crimes of violence, and certain public corruption/abuse-of-office offenses. The 4th District Attorneys expungement overview also treats misdemeanor and felony nonadjudication as a separate expungement pathway after successful completion. ("https://msdeltada.com/expungement/")
Wilma should ask:

Was this a nonadjudication, deferred plea, or withheld adjudication where the court dismissed the case after you completed conditions?

Path H - Pretrial intervention / diversion
Under Mississippis pretrial intervention statutes, successful completion results in a noncriminal disposition of the pending charge or charges. If the person violates the agreement, prosecution resumes. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-15/pretrial-intervention-program/section-99-15-123/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma should ask:

Did the district attorney place you in a pretrial intervention or diversion program, and did you successfully complete it?

If yes:

Route to dismissal/noncriminal-disposition expungement. Get the completion paperwork and dismissal/noncriminal disposition.

Path I - Controlled-substance conditional discharge / drug possession nonadjudication
Under 41-29-150, a first-time eligible drug possession or paraphernalia case may be handled without entering a judgment of guilt. After successful probation/discharge and dismissal, the person may apply to the court for expungement, while a nonpublic record can be retained for future eligibility checks. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-41/chapter-29/article-3/section-41-29-150/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma should ask:

Was this a first-time controlled-substance possession or paraphernalia case where the court deferred proceedings and later dismissed the case?

Path J - Intervention court completion
Mississippi intervention court completion can result in expungement. Under 9-23-23, if the participant completes all intervention court requirements, including fines and fees not waived by the court, the charge and prosecution are dismissed. If the person was sentenced at the time of the guilty plea, successful completion of the intervention court order and probation/suspended-sentence requirements results in the criminal conviction or adjudication being expunged. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-9/chapter-23/section-9-23-23/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma should ask:

Did you complete drug court, veterans court, mental health court, or another Mississippi intervention court?

If yes:

Get the intervention court completion order and the dismissal/expungement order status.

Path K - First-offense DUI expungement
Mississippi has a special first-offense DUI expungement rule. A person convicted of a first-offense DUI may petition the circuit court of the county of conviction at least 5 years after successful completion of all sentence terms, if strict conditions are met. The person must not have held a CDL or commercial learners permit at the time of the offense, must not have refused the blood/breath test, must have had a BAC below .16 if test results are available, must have no other DUI conviction or pending DUI, must justify expungement, and must not have previously had a DUI nonadjudication or expungement. The Mississippi Attorney General has also stated that a municipal court judge is not authorized to expunge a DUI conviction, although municipal or justice courts may handle expungement of nonadjudicated first-offense DUI charges in certain circumstances. ("https://attorneygenerallynnfitch.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/P.Booker_September-29-2020-Expungement-of-Drug-Related-DUIs.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma should treat DUI as a special path:

Do not route a DUI through ordinary misdemeanor expungement. Use the first-offense DUI rule and verify CDL, refusal, BAC, prior DUI, pending DUI, and court type.

Path L - DUI nonadjudication
Mississippi DPS explains that DUI nonadjudication means the court withholds adjudication and sentencing and places the person into a nonadjudication program. A person is eligible for nonadjudication only once under any law authorizing nonadjudication, must successfully complete all court-imposed terms, and must not have held a CDL or commercial learners permit at the time of the offense. ("https://www.driverservicebureau.dps.ms.gov/DriverRecords/DUI_Department?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma should ask:

Was the DUI nonadjudicated instead of resulting in a conviction?

If yes, route differently from DUI conviction expungement.

Path M - Minor in possession / underage alcohol
Under 67-3-70, Mississippi has a special path for underage purchase or possession of light wine, light spirit product, or beer. External clearinghouse guidance summarizes that records relating to underage alcohol purchase/possession can be expunged by the court one year after sentence completion. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-67/chapter-3/section-67-3-70/?utm_source=chatgpt.com") ("https://nationalreentryresourcecenter.org/cleanslate/states/mississippi/policies/ms-c-7?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma should ask:

Was this a minor-in-possession or underage alcohol purchase/possession case?

Path N - Human-trafficking-survivor expungement and vacatur
HB 1546 creates important trafficking-survivor relief effective July 1, 2026. It allows a court to vacate certain convictions or juvenile adjudications connected to victimization, and it allows expungement of a misdemeanor or felony offense not classified as a crime of violence under 97-3-2 if the court finds by a preponderance of the evidence that the persons participation occurred during trafficking and was a direct result of being trafficked. Official documentation creates a presumption but is not required. ("https://legiscan.com/MS/text/HB1546/2026")
Wilma should handle this gently and escalate:

If the charge happened while you were being trafficked or controlled by someone else, you may have a special survivor relief pathway. You do not need to share details you are uncomfortable sharing here; this should be reviewed with a lawyer or survivor legal services.

sourceSections.title: Filing Fee
sourceSections.text:
Mississippi Code 99-19-72 sets a $150 filing fee for each petition to expunge an offense under 99-19-71. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-72/?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma should say:

Expect at least a $150 statutory filing fee for many 99-19-71 petitions, but confirm with the clerk because filing method and local court practice may affect total cost.

sourceSections.title: Effects and Limits of Mississippi Expungement
sourceSections.text:
Mississippis effect language is strong: an expungement restores the person, in contemplation of law, to the status occupied before the arrest or indictment for which convicted, and the person is generally not guilty of perjury or false statement for failing to disclose the expunged arrest, indictment, or conviction, except for statutory exceptions like future first-offender determinations and certain in-camera juror disclosures. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/mississippi/title-99/chapter-19/in-general/section-99-19-71/")
Wilma should still not overpromise. Say:

An expungement can remove the case from public records, but it may not remove every private background-check copy, federal record, immigration consequence, professional licensing issue, driver-history issue, or nonpublic law-enforcement record.

sourceSections.title: Wilma Intake Script
sourceSections.text:
Opening

I can help you understand Mississippi expungement pathways. This is legal information, not legal advice. Mississippi has different rules for dismissals, acquittals, misdemeanors, felonies, nonadjudication, pretrial intervention, intervention court, DUI, underage alcohol, and trafficking-survivor relief.

Core Questions
1 Was the case in Mississippi?
2 Which county and court handled it?
Justice Court?
Municipal Court?
County Court?
Circuit Court?
Youth Court?
3 What is the case number?
4 What was the charge and statute?
5 Was it a misdemeanor, felony, DUI, drug possession, alcohol/MIP, or something else?
6 What was the final outcome?
Dismissed?
Dropped?
No disposition?
Not guilty?
Nonadjudication?
Pretrial intervention?
Intervention court?
Conviction?
7 If convicted, when were all sentence terms completed?
8 Are all fines, costs, restitution, and fees paid or waived?
9 Are there any pending charges?
10 Was this your first offense?
11 Have you ever had a felony expunged before?
12 Was this a traffic violation?
13 Was it a DUI?
14 If DUI, was it first offense, did you refuse testing, was BAC under .16, did you have a CDL or commercial learners permit, and do you have any other DUI pending/conviction?
15 Was it a crime of violence, sex-offense registration, arson, drug trafficking, firearm offense, witness intimidation, vulnerable-person abuse, embezzlement, voyeurism, or prostitution-related felony?
16 Were you a public official and was the conviction related to official duties?
17 Did the case occur while you were being trafficked, coerced, or controlled by someone else?
18 Do you need this for employment, housing, licensing, immigration, school, childcare, healthcare, firearms, public office, or military purposes?

sourceSections.title: Wilma Decision Tree
sourceSections.text:
If dismissed, dropped, no disposition, or not guilty
Route:

Potential mandatory non-conviction expungement under 99-15-26(5) and/or 99-19-71(4). Get disposition proof and file in the originating court.

If misdemeanor not charged/prosecuted within 12 months
Route:

Potential misdemeanor non-prosecution expungement under 99-15-59. Confirm 12 months have passed from arrest/citation.

If non-traffic misdemeanor first offender
Route:

Potential first-offender misdemeanor expungement under 99-19-71(1). Confirm no prior convictions and file in convicting court.

If additional justice/municipal court misdemeanors
Route:

Potential discretionary expungement under 9-11-15 or 21-23-7. Need rehabilitation, 2 years good conduct since last conviction, prosecutor notice, and best-interest-of-society showing.

If felony conviction
Route:

Check one-felony lifetime rule, sentence completion, fines/costs paid, waiting period, rehabilitation, DA notice, and exclusion list. Use 5-year rule before July 1, 2026; use 3-year rule on or after July 1, 2026.

If first-offense DUI conviction
Route:

Use DUI-specific expungement. Confirm 5 years after sentence completion, no CDL/CLP at offense, no refusal, BAC below .16 if available, no other DUI conviction or pending DUI, justification, and no prior DUI nonadjudication/expungement.

If DUI nonadjudication
Route:

Use DUI nonadjudication completion path, not DUI conviction path.

If pretrial intervention
Route:

Confirm successful completion and noncriminal disposition. Route to dismissal/noncriminal-disposition expungement.

If intervention court
Route:

Confirm completion of all intervention court requirements and whether conviction/adjudication was expunged or still needs order processing.

If drug possession conditional discharge
Route:

Check 41-29-150 first-time drug possession/paraphernalia pathway. Confirm discharge/dismissal and one-time eligibility.

If minor-in-possession alcohol
Route:

Check 67-3-70. Potential petition one year after sentence completion.

If trafficking-survivor facts
Route:

Potential survivor relief effective July 1, 2026 under amended 97-3-54.6. Attorney or survivor legal services review required.

sourceSections.title: Mandatory Attorney Escalation Flags
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should recommend attorney or legal-clinic review if any of these apply:
Felony conviction.
Any felony near the excluded list.
Crime of violence under 97-3-2.
Sex offender registration or failure to register.
DUI, especially CDL, refusal, BAC .16+, prior DUI, pending DUI, or felony DUI.
Human trafficking survivor relief.
Public official conviction related to official duties.
Embezzlement.
Firearm offense.
Drug trafficking.
Witness intimidation.
Vulnerable-person abuse, neglect, or exploitation.
Prostitution-related felony after July 1, 2026.
Pending charge.
Unpaid restitution, fines, or costs.
Immigration or noncitizen issue.
Firearms rights issue.
Professional licensing, healthcare, childcare, teaching, law enforcement, or government employment.
Federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state record.
User wants guaranteed relief.

9. Wilma
Do Not Say
Rules
Wilma should not say:

Your record is erased from everywhere.

Nobody can ever see it again.

This clears FBI records.

This fixes immigration.

This restores gun rights.

Every felony can be expunged.

Every misdemeanor can be expunged automatically.

DUI is treated like a normal misdemeanor.

The felony waiting period is already 3 years before July 1, 2026.

A pretrial intervention or nonadjudication always clears automatically without checking the court record.

Private background-check companies will update automatically.

Wilma may say:

Based on what you shared, you may have a Mississippi expungement pathway. The next step is to verify the court record, disposition, sentence completion, payment status, prosecutor notice requirements, and whether any exclusion or special pathway applies.

sourceSections.title: Filing Packet Checklist
sourceSections.text:
For a Mississippi expungement packet, Wilma should prepare or request:
Full legal name.
Date of birth.
Current address.
Phone and email.
County.
Court name.
Case number.
Charge name.
Mississippi Code section, if known.
Arrest or citation date.
Disposition date.
Conviction date, if any.
Sentence completion date.
Probation completion proof.
Intervention/pretrial/nonadjudication completion proof.
Sentencing order, if convicted.
Disposition order, dismissal order, or not-guilty judgment.
Account balance sheet showing zero balance.
Proof fines, costs, restitution, and fees paid or waived.
Prior conviction history.
Pending charge check.
Prior felony expungement history.
Prior DUI nonadjudication/expungement history, if DUI.
Prosecutor/district attorney notice.
Petition for expungement.
Proposed order of expungement.
Certified copies after order is signed.
Agency distribution list: clerk, Mississippi Criminal Information Center, arresting agency, prosecutor, jail/sheriff, municipal/justice/county/circuit court as applicable.

sourceSections.title: Mississippi Quick Reference
sourceSections.text:
Topic Mississippi Rule
Main conviction statute 99-19-71
Dismissed/dropped/no disposition/not guilty Petition; court shall expunge
Misdemeanor no charge/prosecution Petition after 12 months under 99-15-59
First-offender misdemeanor Non-traffic misdemeanor conviction may be expunged
Additional justice/municipal misdemeanors Discretionary; 2 years good conduct + rehabilitation
Felony conviction One felony conviction; sentence complete; fines/costs paid; court finds rehabilitation
Felony waiting period now 5 years after sentence completion
Felony waiting period effective July 1, 2026 3 years after sentence completion
General felony exclusions Violence, first-degree arson, drug trafficking, 3rd+ DUI, felon firearm, sex registration failure, voyeurism, witness intimidation, vulnerable-person abuse, embezzlement
New July 1, 2026 felony exclusions Felony procuring prostitution, promoting prostitution
Public official bar No expungement for conviction related to official duties
DA notice for felony 10 days written notice before hearing
Pretrial intervention Successful completion creates noncriminal disposition
Intervention court Completion may result in dismissal and expungement
First-offense DUI Special 5-year rule with strict conditions
DUI nonadjudication Separate one-time program
MIP alcohol Potential petition one year after sentence completion
Filing fee $150 for 99-19-71 petitions
Statewide form No single statewide official form identified; confirm with originating court clerk
Legal aid resources ExpungeMississippi, Mississippi Center for Justice, MVLP
